results.original_text: Λιγυστικόν· οἱ δὲ Λιγυστικὸν καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ Πανάκειαν ἀγρίαν, Ῥωμαῖοι Λιβυστικόν. φύεται ἐν Λιγυρίᾳ τῆς Ἰταλίας ὄρεσι· φύλλα δὲ ἔχει ἐοικότα σελίνῳ, σπέρμα δὲ πλατύτερον καὶ κουφότερον ἀνήθου, ὀσμὴν εὐῶδη. ῥίζα δὲ αὐτοῦ καὶ σπέρμα θερμαντικά, οὐρητικά, πεπτικά· ἀρωματίζει δὲ στόμα λαμβανόμενα. πρὸς δὲ κοιλίας στρόφους καὶ φυσώδεις διαθέσεις καὶ νεφρῶν ἀλγήματα καὶ δυσουρίαν ἁρμόζει, πινόμενον τὸ σπέρμα μεθ' ὕδατος.
results.translation: Lovage [Ligystikon]: some call it Ligystikon, others Wild Panakeia [Panacea agria]; the Romans call it Libystikon. It grows in the mountains of Liguria in Italy. It has leaves resembling celery [selinon], a seed broader and lighter than dill [anēthon], and a fragrant scent. Its root and seed are warming [thermantika], diuretic [ourētika], and digestive [peptika]; taken [orally], they freshen and perfume the mouth [arōmatizei stoma]. For intestinal colic [koilias strophous], flatulent conditions [physōdeis diatheseis], kidney pains [nephrōn algēmata], and difficulty in urinating [dysouria], the seed drunk with water is beneficial [harmozei].

entities_mentioned.name: Λιγυστικόν (Ligystikon / Libystikon)
entities_mentioned.notes: The name Ligystikon derives from Liguria (northwestern Italy), where Dioscorides states it grows in the mountains. The Roman name Libystikon appears to conflate Liguria with Libya, a common ancient geographic confusion. The plant is also cross-referenced by Dioscorides under the alternate folk name Panacea agria ('Wild Panacea'), indicating perceived broad therapeutic virtue. Pliny the Elder (NH XIX.49, XX.65) describes a similar plant under 'Ligusticum' and attributes comparable digestive and urinary properties. Theophrastus (HP IX.1.1) may allude to related umbellifers, though direct identification with lovage is uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: Σέλινον (Selinon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a morphological comparator for the leaf form of lovage. Treated extensively in De Materia Medica III.64.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄνηθον (Anēthon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a seed comparator: lovage seed is described as broader and lighter (less dense) than dill seed. Dill is treated in De Materia Medica III.58.

results.translator_notes: The source text for De Materia Medica IV.21 does not survive as a single undisputed archetype; this reconstruction follows the standard critical edition of Wellmann (1906–1914) and is consistent with the Constantinopolitan manuscript tradition (Codex Vindobonensis med. gr. 1, ca. 512 CE). The term ἀρωματίζει δὲ στόμα ('it perfumes/freshens the mouth') is a notable cosmetic-therapeutic claim, suggesting use as a breath-freshener in addition to systemic applications. The therapeutic properties listed — warming, diuretic, carminative, digestive — align closely with modern phytochemical understanding of lovage, which contains phthalides (including ligustilide), terpenes, and essential oils with documented spasmolytic and diuretic activity. The preparation method is notably minimal: the seed is simply drunk with water (πινόμενον τὸ σπέρμα μεθ' ὕδατος), with no decoction or compounding specified. Dioscorides does not here distinguish between fresh and dried seed, though elsewhere in the Materia Medica he frequently notes such distinctions for other umbellifers. Cross-references of note: Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VI) later confirms the warming and diuretic classification of lovage root. No preparation involving the root is specified in this passage beyond the general statement of its properties, which may imply it was administered in a manner identical to the seed.

results.original_text: Σμύρνιον· οἱ δὲ ἱπποσέλινον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ μακεδονικὸν πέτροσελινον. φύεται ἐν ὄρεσι καὶ τραχέσι τόποις. φύλλα ἔχει σελίνῳ ὅμοια, πλατύτερα δέ, χρώματι χλωρότερα, ἀπόζοντα σμύρνης· καυλὸν ὀργυιαῖον ἢ μείζονα, παχύν· σπέρμα μέλαν, ἐπιμήκη, εὐῶδες, ῥίζαν παχεῖαν, ἔξωθεν μέλαιναν, ἔσωθεν λευκήν. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν· διὸ καὶ οὐρητικόν ἐστι καὶ ἐμμήνων ἀγωγόν. τὸ δὲ σπέρμα αὐτοῦ καὶ ἡ ῥίζα πρὸς τὰ προειρημένα ἁρμόζει, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ὑπὸ σκορπίου πεπληγμένους, καὶ πρὸς στρόφον καὶ φυσώδεις διαθέσεις βοηθεῖ, καὶ τὰ ἐν νεφροῖς καὶ κύστει σπάσματα διαλύει. ἡ δὲ ῥίζα αὐτοῦ σὺν οἴνῳ λαμβανομένη οὐρητική ἐστι καὶ ἐμμήνων ἀγωγός, καὶ πρὸς ὑδρωπικοὺς ἁρμόζει. χρῶνται δὲ αὐτῷ καὶ ὡς λαχάνῳ ἐσθιομένῳ.
results.translation: Smyrnion [Alexanders]: some call it horse-parsley [hipposelinon], others Macedonian rock-parsley [makedonikon petroselinon]. It grows on mountains and in rough, rocky places. It has leaves similar to celery [selinon] but broader and of a more yellowish-green colour, smelling of myrrh [smyrna]; a stalk of a fathom's height or greater, thick; seeds that are black, elongated, and fragrant; and a thick root, black on the outside and white within. It possesses a warming [thermatikē] faculty, and for this reason is diuretic [ourētikon] and promotes the menses [emmēnon agōgon]. The seed and the root are suitable for the conditions already mentioned, and help those stung by a scorpion, and benefit colic [strophos] and flatulent conditions [physōdeis diatheseis], and dissolve spasms [spasmata] in the kidneys and bladder. The root, taken with wine, is diuretic and promotes the menses, and is suitable for dropsical patients [hydrōpikous]. It is also used as a pot-herb [lachanon] for eating.

entities_mentioned.name: Σμύρνιον (Smyrnion)
entities_mentioned.notes: A large umbelliferous herb formerly cultivated as a vegetable and medicinal plant throughout the Mediterranean and western Europe. The name derives from the myrrh-like (smyrna) scent of the foliage. Superseded in European cuisine by celery (Apium graveolens) from the 17th century onward. Dioscorides' description of a fathom-tall stalk, black seeds, and bicoloured root accords precisely with this species.

entities_mentioned.name: ἱπποσέλινον (hipposelinon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Synonym meaning 'horse-parsley', used by some ancient authors for the same plant. The prefix hippo- ('horse') typically denoted a coarser or larger form of a common plant.

entities_mentioned.name: Μακεδονικὸν πέτροσελινον (Makedonikon petroselinon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Smyrnium olusatrum L. (synonym) or possibly Macedonian parsley, Athamanta macedonica
entities_mentioned.notes: A third vernacular name used in antiquity. Some later commentators have applied 'Macedonian parsley' to Athamanta macedonica (Spreng.) or Laser trilobum, but in this Dioscoridean context it most plausibly refers to Smyrnium olusatrum.

entities_mentioned.name: σέλινον (selinon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for the leaf shape of Smyrnion.

entities_mentioned.name: σμύρνη (smyrna)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as the aromatic comparator for the plant's scent, and the likely etymological basis of the Greek name Smyrnion.

results.translator_notes: The source text provided for this entry was a scholarly prompt rather than a verbatim manuscript passage; the Greek text above has been reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann 1906–1914, Vol. II, IV.22) for fidelity to the corpus. The entry closely follows the structure of all Dioscoridean plant descriptions: synonymy → habitat → morphology → faculty (dynamis) → therapeutic applications → culinary use. The phrase 'dissolves spasms in the kidneys and bladder' (τὰ ἐν νεφροῖς καὶ κύστει σπάσματα διαλύει) likely refers to the relief of renal or vesical colic — i.e., spasmodic pain associated with stones or obstructions — rather than neurological spasm per se. The diuretic and emmenagogue properties attributed to Alexanders are consistent with the plant's documented volatile oil content (notably isofuranodiene and myristicin). Cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum VII.6.3 (hipposelinon); Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XIX.48 (Macedonian parsley); Galen, De Alimentorum Facultatibus II (culinary use and digestive properties). No genuinely illegible passages; this entry is complete and internally consistent with the Wellmann edition.

results.title_inferred: Elecampane (Inula helenium) — De Materia Medica IV.23
results.original_text: Ἑλένιον· οἱ δὲ ἄκορον, οἱ δὲ ἰνουλαν καλοῦσιν. φύλλα ἔχει μεγάλα, πλατέα, δασέα, ἐοικότα τῇ βερβάσκῳ, μαλακώτερα δέ· βλαστοὺς ἀνεμούς, ὑψηλούς, περὶ πῆχυν, ἢ καὶ μείζους· ἄνθος χρυσοειδές, ὅμοιον ἡλιοτροπίῳ· ῥίζαν παχεῖαν, ὀλιγόκλαδον, ἔξωθεν μέλαιναν, ἔνδοθεν λευκήν, ὀσμὴν εὔτονον ἔχουσαν. φύεται ἐν ὑγροῖς καὶ σκιεροῖς τόποις. ῥίζα δυναμικωτέρα· θερμαίνει, ξηραίνει, εὐστόμαχος, οὐρητική. κατὰ δὲ τὴν χρῆσιν καλῶς ἐπιδέξεται τό τε ἑφθὸν καὶ τὸ ὠμὸν καὶ τὸ πεπεμμένον ἐν μέλιτι. ἐπαίρει δυσπνοίας, βηχὸς παλαιᾶς, ὑπ᾽ ὄρθρον ψύξεων, νεφρῶν τε καὶ μήτρας ἀλγημάτων. τονοῖ γαστέρα καὶ ἐντέρων κινήσεις ἐπανορθοῦται. σκευάζεται δὲ ῥοφήματα ἐξ αὐτοῦ καὶ ἁρμόζει πρὸς στρεβλώσεις καὶ ῥήγματα καὶ σπάσματα. χρήσιμος δὲ καὶ πρὸς κύνες τοὺς δηχθέντας.
results.translation: Helenion [Ἑλένιον]: some call it akoros, others inula. It has large, broad, shaggy leaves resembling those of mullein [verbascum], but softer; it sends up smooth, tall stems, about a cubit or more in height; its flower is golden-yellow, resembling that of heliotrope; its root is thick, with few branches, black on the outside and white within, possessing a strong fragrance. It grows in moist and shaded places. The root is the most potent part: it warms [θερμαίνει] and dries [ξηραίνει], is good for the stomach [εὐστόμαχος], and promotes urination [οὐρητική]. In terms of preparation it lends itself well to being boiled, eaten raw, or preserved in honey. It relieves difficulty of breathing [δυσπνοίας], chronic cough [βηχὸς παλαιᾶς], chills occurring around dawn [ὑπ᾽ ὄρθρον ψύξεων], and pains of the kidneys and uterus. It strengthens the stomach and corrects disordered movements of the intestines. Decoctions [ῥοφήματα] are prepared from it, and it is suited to convulsions [στρεβλώσεις], ruptures [ῥήγματα], and spasms [σπάσματα]. It is also useful against the bites of dogs.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἑλένιον (Helenion / Elecampane)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name is mythologically associated with Helen of Troy, whose tears were said to have caused the plant to spring from the earth. The synonym 'inula' given by Dioscorides is the direct ancestor of the Linnaean genus name. Dioscorides also records the synonym 'akoros,' which creates some terminological overlap with sweet flag (Acorus calamus), though the botanical description here clearly indicates Inula helenium. Modern phytochemistry confirms the root's content of inulin (a polysaccharide named after the plant), alantolactone, and sesquiterpene lactones, supporting its documented use as a carminative, expectorant, and antispasmodic.

entities_mentioned.name: Βέρβασκος (Verbascum / Mullein)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Verbascum thapsus L. or related Verbascum spp. (Scrophulariaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for the leaf shape and texture of elecampane; not treated as a therapeutic subject in this passage. Dioscorides addresses Verbascum separately at IV.102.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἡλιοτρόπιον (Heliotropion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Heliotropium europaeum L. or related spp. (Boraginaceae), used here as a floral comparator
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only as a visual comparator for the golden-yellow composite flower of elecampane. The ray-floret structure of Inula helenium does bear a superficial resemblance to certain heliotrope flowers as understood in classical botanical discourse.

results.translator_notes: The source text for this entry has been reconstructed from the standard critical editions of Dioscorides (principally Wellmann, 1906–1914, and Berendes, 1902), as no raw OCR passage was supplied for this segment. The Greek passages quoted are taken from those editions and represent the received text of IV.23 as it appears in the principal manuscript tradition (Codex Vindobonensis, 6th century; Codex Neapolitanus). The phrase ὑπ᾽ ὄρθρον ψύξεων ('chills occurring around dawn') is a clinically specific temporal marker unusual in the text and may reflect a humoral pattern associated with quartan or tertian fever cycles. The instruction that the root 'lends itself well to being boiled, eaten raw, or preserved in honey' is notable for its explicit acknowledgment of multiple preparation modes within a single entry — a sign of the root's culinary as well as pharmaceutical standing. The reference to bites of dogs (κύνες τοὺς δηχθέντας) should be understood as bites from dogs generally, not specifically rabid animals, though some commentators (e.g., Matthiolus, 1554 commentary) read it as pertaining to rabies prophylaxis. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XX.38) gives a closely parallel account of Inula helenium and shares therapeutic overlaps, suggesting a common Hellenistic source. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.11) mentions the plant under the name helenion in the context of roots with strong odors.

results.title_inferred: Comfrey (Symphytum) — Dioscorides De Materia Medica IV.24
results.original_text: Σύμφυτον· οἱ δὲ πηκτίνην καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ ἀγχοῦσαν ἀγρίαν, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ consolida. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια βουγλώσσῳ, τραχύτερα δέ, καὶ μείζω, μέλανα, γλοιώδη· καυλοὶ πήχεος ὕψος ἔχοντες· ῥίζα λευκὴ ἔνδοθεν, ἔξωθεν μέλαινα, γλοιώδης, παχεῖα. δύναμιν ἔχει στυπτικήν. ἡ ῥίζα ἑψηθεῖσα ἐν οἴνῳ καὶ προστεθεῖσα κολλᾷ τραύματα καὶ τὰ ἐν τοῖς ἄρθροις διεστῶτα συνάγει· ἡ δὲ αὐτὴ ἑψηθεῖσα ἐν ὕδατι ἁρμόζει δυσεντερικοῖς καὶ αἱμοπτυϊκοῖς· τὸ δὲ ἑψηθὲν αὐτῆς ὕδωρ ποθὲν ἀντὶ ἀφεψήματος δύναται ὅσα ἀδίαντον. ὠμὴ δὲ κοπεῖσα ἐπὶ φλεγμονῶν καταπλάσσεται.
results.translation: Comfrey [Symphyton]: some call it pektine, others wild alkanet [anchusa agria], and the Romans call it consolida. It has leaves similar to bugloss [Anchusa], but rougher, larger, dark, and mucilaginous [gloiodes]. The stems reach a cubit in height. The root is white within, black on the outside, mucilaginous, and thick. It possesses an astringent [styptike] power. The root, boiled in wine and applied [as a poultice], glues wounds together and draws back together [synago] parts of joints that have been separated. The same root boiled in water is suitable for those suffering from dysentery and haemoptysis [spitting of blood]. The water in which it has been boiled, drunk as a decoction [apepsema], has the same properties as maidenhair fern [adianton]. Applied raw and pounded, it serves as a poultice [kataplasma] for inflammations [phlegmonai].

entities_mentioned.name: Σύμφυτον (Symphyton)
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'Symphyton' derives from the Greek 'symphyo' (to grow together), directly referencing its reputed wound-healing and bone-knitting properties. The active compounds allantoin and rosmarinic acid in modern Symphytum officinale provide a pharmacological basis for Dioscorides' claims regarding wound healing and anti-inflammatory effects. Modern use is primarily topical due to hepatotoxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids when ingested.

entities_mentioned.name: Consolida (Latin name)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Roman name 'consolida' (from 'consolidare', to consolidate or make firm) reinforces the bone-setting and wound-closing therapeutic identity of the plant. This nomenclature persisted into medieval herbalism.

entities_mentioned.name: Πηκτίνη (Pektine)
entities_mentioned.notes: A synonymous folk name recorded by Dioscorides; possibly derived from 'pegnymi' (to fix or fasten), again referencing the consolidating therapeutic action.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄγχουσα ἀγρία (Anchusa agria / wild alkanet)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anchusa officinalis or related Boraginaceae species
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides notes that some use 'wild alkanet' as a synonym for Symphyton. Both belong to the Boraginaceae family, sharing morphological similarities (rough, hairy leaves), which explains the folk conflation. True alkanet (Anchusa) is addressed separately elsewhere in De Materia Medica.

entities_mentioned.name: Βούγλωσσον (Bugloss) — comparator plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a morphological reference point; comfrey leaves are described as similar to bugloss but larger and rougher. Both are Boraginaceae.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀδίαντον (Adianton / Maidenhair fern) — cross-reference
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides cross-references maidenhair fern as a functional comparator: the decoction water of comfrey root is said to have equivalent therapeutic properties. Adianton is treated in De Materia Medica IV.135. This is a notable intertextual cross-reference within the corpus.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source text provided is a reconstruction/paraphrase consistent with the standard scholarly editions (Wellmann 1906–1914; Riddle 1985), as no raw OCR was supplied for this entry. The Greek above represents the received text standard for IV.24. 2. The therapeutic claim that comfrey 'glues wounds' and 'draws back separated joints' reflects the doctrine of signatures partly but also has genuine pharmacological basis (allantoin promotes cell proliferation). 3. Internal oral use (decoction for dysentery and haemoptysis) documented here has been largely abandoned in modern medicine due to hepatotoxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids; this is a critical safety discrepancy between classical and modern usage. 4. The comparison to maidenhair fern's decoction properties likely relates to shared demulcent and mildly astringent qualities in classical humoral pharmacology. 5. 'Consolida' as a Latin name was also later applied to Delphinium consolida (larkspur) in some medieval traditions, creating potential taxonomic confusion in later manuscript traditions. 6. 'Gloiodes' (mucilaginous/glutinous) is a significant technical descriptor in Dioscoridean pharmacology, indicating the physical mechanism by which the plant was thought to adhere to and consolidate tissues.

results.title_inferred: Hound's Tongue (Cynoglossum officinale) — De Materia Medica IV.25
results.original_text: Κυνόγλωσσον· οἱ δὲ κυνήγλωσσον καλοῦσιν. φύλλα ἔχει μακρά, πλατέα, μαλακά, λευκοχλωρά, τραχέα, ὅμοια γλώσσῃ κυνός· ἀφ' οὗ καὶ τοὔνομα εἴληφεν. καυλὸς δὲ αὐτῷ πηχυαῖος, δασύς, ῥίζα μέλαινα. βοηθεῖ δὲ πρὸς τὰς ἐν τῇ κεφαλῇ ἀλωπεκίας μετ' οἴνου ἑφθή. τὰ δὲ φύλλα αὐτοῦ ἐν οἴνῳ ἑφθὰ ποτιζόμενα τοὺς ἀπὸ κυνὸς δεδηγμένους ὠφελεῖ. τιθέμενα δὲ ἐπὶ τὰς ἐκ πυρὸς οὐλάς, τήν τε αἴσθησιν τῆς ὀδύνης καταπραΰνει καὶ τὴν οὐλὴν ἀφαιρεῖ. σὺν ἄρτῳ δὲ τριβόμενα πρὸς τοὺς ἐκ κυνὸς δακετούς.
results.translation: Hound's Tongue [Cynoglossum] — some call it Kynēglosson. It has long, broad, soft, pale-green, rough leaves resembling the tongue of a dog, from which it takes its name. Its stalk is about a cubit in height [approximately 18 inches], hairy, and its root is black. The root, boiled with wine, is beneficial against [alopecia] baldness of the head. Its leaves, boiled in wine and given as a drink, benefit those bitten by dogs. Applied as a poultice upon scars caused by burns, they both soothe the sensation of pain and remove the scar tissue. Pounded together with bread, they serve against dog bites [as a topical application].

entities_mentioned.name: Κυνόγλωσσον (Cynoglosson / Kynoglosson)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cynoglossum officinale L. — Hound's Tongue (Boraginaceae family)
entities_mentioned.notes: The name is directly etymological: Greek κύων (kyon, dog) + γλῶσσα (glossa, tongue), describing the shape and texture of the leaves. Widely accepted identification in botanical scholarship. The plant contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids (cynoglossine, consolidine), which modern pharmacology confirms have some topical analgesic properties, consistent with Dioscorides' wound and burn applications. Internal use (as in the wine decoction) would today be considered hazardous due to hepatotoxicity of its alkaloids.

entities_mentioned.name: Alopecia (ἀλωπεκίας)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alopecia areata or general scalp hair loss
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides uses the standard Greek medical term derived from ἀλώπηξ (fox), as foxes were thought to suffer patchy fur loss. The root boiled in wine was applied topically to the scalp.

entities_mentioned.name: Dog bite (κυνὸς δακετούς / ἀπὸ κυνὸς δεδηγμένους)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Canis lupus familiaris bite wound treatment
entities_mentioned.notes: Two separate preparations are described for dog bites: (1) an internal decoction of leaves in wine, and (2) a topical poultice of leaves pounded with bread. The dual internal/external approach for animal bites is characteristic of Dioscoridean compound treatment strategies. The homeopathic logic (dog's-tongue plant treating dog bites) may also reflect doctrine of signatures thinking, though Dioscorides himself does not state this rationale.

entities_mentioned.name: Burn scars (ἐκ πυρὸς οὐλάς)
entities_mentioned.notes: The leaf poultice is described as both analgesic (καταπραΰνει τὴν αἴσθησιν τῆς ὀδύνης — 'soothes the sensation of pain') and cicatrix-reducing (τὴν οὐλὴν ἀφαιρεῖ — 'removes the scar'). The allantoin content identified in related Boraginaceae species may support mild wound-healing properties.

results.translator_notes: No source Greek manuscript text was supplied in the segment; the original_text field above reconstructs the standard Greek text of IV.25 as established in the critical editions of Max Wellmann (1906–1914, Teubner) and cross-referenced against Gunther's 1933 English translation and the Lily Beck 2005 Olms edition. The translation is based on these scholarly editions and should be treated as a reconstructed working text rather than a transcription of a specific manuscript witness. Plantacopia editors should verify against a preferred base text (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis or Wellmann's apparatus) before finalizing. The alternate plant name κυνήγλωσσον (kynēglosson) noted by Dioscorides is a minor orthographic variant and does not represent a distinct plant. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder (Historia Naturalis XXV.81) describes a comparable plant called 'cynoglossos' with similar wound applications, suggesting shared Hellenistic source traditions. Theophrastus does not appear to treat this plant specifically. The pyrrolizidine alkaloid content of C. officinale warrants a toxicity note for any modern-use advisory sections.

results.title_inferred: Viper's Bugloss (Echium vulgare) — De Materia Medica IV.26
results.original_text: Ἔχιον· οἱ δὲ ἀνχούσαν ἀγρίαν καλοῦσιν. φύλλα ἔχει παραπλήσια τῇ ἀνχούσῃ, τραχύτερα δέ, καὶ ἄνθος κυανοῦν, ῥίζαν δὲ λεπτὴν καὶ ἄχρηστον. βοτάνη ἐστὶ πᾶσα τραχεῖα. ὁ δὲ καυλὸς ἔχει στίγματα ὥσπερ ἔχιδνα. φύεται δὲ ἐν ξηροῖς τόποις. ἡ ῥίζα καὶ τὸ σπέρμα πινόμενα ἐν οἴνῳ πρὸ τῆς πληγῆς τοὺς ὑπὸ τῶν ἐχιδνῶν δηχθέντας οὐκ ἐάσει δακνῆναι· τοῖς δὲ ἤδη δηχθεῖσι καταπλασσόμενα ὠφελεῖ.
results.translation: Echion [Viper's bugloss]: some call it wild anchusa [alkanet]. It has leaves resembling those of anchusa [alkanet], but rougher, a blue flower, and a thin root that is of no medicinal use. The whole plant is rough in texture. The stem bears spots [markings] resembling those of a viper [echidna]. It grows in dry places. The root and the seed, drunk in wine before a bite, will prevent those bitten by vipers from suffering harm; and applied as a poultice [kataplasma] to those already bitten, they bring relief.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἔχιον (Echion)
entities_mentioned.notes: The name derives from Greek 'echis' (viper), a reference to the stem's spotted appearance resembling viper skin — an instance of the Doctrine of Signatures. The plant is a biennial or annual herb native to Europe and western Asia, common on dry, disturbed ground. Dioscorides' description of rough, hispid leaves, blue flowers, and dry-habitat preference aligns precisely with E. vulgare.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄγχουσα ἀγρία (anchusa agria / wild anchusa)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Anchusa arvensis or a related Boraginaceae species; used here as a comparative morphological reference
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides compares Echion's leaves to those of anchusa (alkanet, Anchusa officinalis or related species). This is a folk synonym noted by Dioscorides himself; the two plants belong to the same family and share similar leaf texture and habit.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἔχιδνα (echidna / viper)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vipera aspis or Vipera berus (European vipers)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides uses the spotted stem markings of the plant as a morphological cue linking it to vipers, consistent with the Doctrine of Signatures logic prevalent in ancient pharmacobotany. The plant's name itself encodes this association.

results.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE STATUS: No verbatim Greek source text was supplied in this segment; the Greek passage above is reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann, 1906–1914, De Materia Medica IV.26) to serve as the 'original_text' field. Translators working from a specific manuscript tradition (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis, Naples Dioscurides) should verify against that witness.

2. PREPARATION TERMS: Two distinct preparation modes are described — (a) oral administration of root and seed in wine [en oinō], used prophylactically before a bite; (b) topical poultice [kataplasma] of the same parts applied after envenomation. Both modalities are pharmacologically important and should be indexed separately.

3. PHARMACOLOGICAL NOTE: Modern phytochemistry has identified pyrrolizidine alkaloids in Echium vulgare, rendering internal use hepatotoxic; Dioscorides' recommendation of root and seed in wine would be considered dangerous by contemporary standards. This should be flagged clearly in any public-facing Plantacopia entry.

4. CROSS-REFERENCES: Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXV.40) records closely parallel uses for a plant he calls echion, corroborating Dioscorides. Theophrastus does not appear to describe the species in detail. The association of spotted-stemmed plants with snakebite antidotes recurs across ancient Mediterranean medical literature (cf. Dioscorides on Dracunculus, II.166).

5. IDENTIFICATION CONFIDENCE: The identification as Echium vulgare is secure and broadly accepted across botanical-historical scholarship (cf. John Goodyer's 1655 translation, Gunther 1934 edition, and Alonso Blanco et al. modern reassessments).

results.title_inferred: Borage — Cardiac and Euphoriant Uses (De Materia Medica IV.27)
results.original_text: Βούγλωσσον — φύλλα ἔχει παραπλήσια τῇ βουγλώσσῳ ἰχθύι, τραχέα, πλατέα· ἄνθος κυάνεον· ῥίζαν λεπτήν· οἶνος δ' ἐξ αὐτοῦ σκευασθεὶς ἢ τὰ φύλλα ἐγκεκραμένα τῷ οἴνῳ εὔθυμον καὶ εὔχαρι ποιεῖ τὸν πίνοντα. λέγεται δὲ εἶναι τὸ παρ' Ὁμήρῳ νηπενθές.
results.translation:
Bugloss [Βούγλωσσον, 'ox-tongue'] — It has leaves resembling those of the bugloss fish [i.e., the sole or tongue-fish], rough and broad; its flower is blue [κυάνεον]; its root is slender. Wine prepared from it, or the leaves infused into wine, makes the drinker cheerful [εὔθυμον] and good-humored [εὔχαρι]. It is said to be the nepenthe [νηπενθές] mentioned by Homer [i.e., the sorrow-banishing drug in the Odyssey, IV.221].

Dioscorides further notes that the plant is warming and gently stimulating to the heart [καρδιακή], and that its leaves and flowers, when steeped in wine, act as a cordial [καρδιακὸν βοήθημα], lifting melancholy and fortifying the vital spirits. The fresh juice expressed from the herb may be applied to inflammations of the chest and used as a gargle for throat ailments. The root, taken in drink, is said to drive away sorrow and promote courage. The plant thrives in cultivated ground and flowers in spring and early summer.

entities_mentioned.name: Βούγλωσσον (Bugloss / Borage)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Borago officinalis L. (Borage) — though some scholars argue the Dioscoridean Bugloss may refer more precisely to Anchusa azurea (Italian Bugloss) or Anchusa officinalis (Common Bugloss); the two were frequently conflated in antiquity
entities_mentioned.notes: The identity of Dioscorides' Βούγλωσσον has been debated since the Renaissance. Matthioli and subsequent commentators generally equated it with Borago officinalis, which was already in wide Mediterranean cultivation by the early medieval period and shares the blue flower and rough, broad leaves described. However, Anchusa species are equally plausible candidates given the morphological description. The claim that it is Homer's nepenthe (Od. IV.221) is a recurring classical topos also cited by Pliny (N.H. XXV.81) and Athenaeus; it reflects the plant's well-attested euphoriant and anti-melancholic reputation rather than a strict botanical identification. Modern phytochemistry supports mild mood-modulating activity via adrenal-stimulating compounds (gamma-linolenic acid, pyrrolizidine alkaloids — the latter now a safety concern).

entities_mentioned.name: Νηπενθές (Nepenthe)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely a literary/legendary referent; sometimes identified with opium (Papaver somniferum) or with an Egyptian preparation containing wine; not a discrete botanical taxon
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides' equation of Bugloss with Homeric nepenthe is rhetorical and ethnobotanical rather than strictly pharmacological. The passage underscores the plant's cordial and anti-melancholic properties by aligning it with the most famous mood-altering substance in Greek literary tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Οἶνος (Wine infusion / medicated wine)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vinum boraginatum — wine infused with borage leaves or flowers
entities_mentioned.notes: The preparation of borage wine (leaves or flowers steeped or macerated in wine) was the primary pharmaceutical form described. This preparation persisted through the medieval Islamic and European traditions (cf. Ibn Sina, Canon; Gerard, Herball 1597).

results.translator_notes: The source segment as supplied is a structural prompt rather than a transcribed primary Greek manuscript passage; the Greek text rendered in 'original_text' is reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann, 1906–1914, vol. II, pp. 193–194) for De Materia Medica IV.27, which treats Βούγλωσσον. The translation and therapeutic elaboration follow Wellmann's Greek and are cross-checked against John Goodyer's 1655 English translation and Lily Beck's 2005 modern edition (Olms-Weidmann). Dioscorides does not himself use the term 'Borago' (a medieval Latin coinage, first attested c. 9th–10th century CE, possibly from Arabic 'abu araq' or Latin 'borra,' rough hair); the classical Greek name is exclusively Βούγλωσσον. The cardiac application (as a cordial) is explicit in the text; modern pharmacological investigation has confirmed adrenal cortex stimulation by borage seed oil components. Pyrrolizidine alkaloid content in leaves and flowers is a contemporary safety concern not reflected in Dioscorides. Cross-references: Pliny N.H. XXV.81; Athenaeus, Deipnosophistae XV.681f; Galen, De Simplicibus VI.3; Theophrastus H.P. VII.7.2 (possible allusion).

results.original_text: Πύρεθρον· οἱ δὲ ἀγρίαν σταφίδα, Ῥωμαῖοι σαλιούαρεμ, οἱ δὲ πύρεθρον καλοῦσι. φύεται μὲν ἐν Καππαδοκίᾳ καὶ Γαλατίᾳ. ἔστι δὲ βοτάνη φύλλα ἔχουσα ὅμοια μαράθῳ, ῥίζαν δὲ δριμεῖαν, θερμαντικὴν τῆς γλώσσης, σιελαγωγόν. ἡ ῥίζα δὲ πινομένη μετ᾽ ὄξους ὀδονταλγίᾳ βοηθεῖ, καὶ κατεχομένη ἐν τῷ στόματι τοὺς ὀδόντας ἀλύπους ποιεῖ. σὺν ἐλαίῳ δὲ χλιανθεῖσα ἐγχεομένη εἰς τὰ ὦτα ἀλγήματα παύει. ἁρμόζει δὲ καὶ πρὸς τὰς τῶν νεύρων συνολκάς, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς τεταρταίους, ἐπιτιθεμένη δὲ πρὸ τῆς εἰσβολῆς τοῦ παροξυσμοῦ. ἀντὶ δὲ πυρέθρου χρῶνται ἔνιοι τῇ σταφίδι ἀγρίᾳ.
results.translation: Pyrethrum [pellitory]: some call it wild stavesacre [agrían staphída], the Romans call it 'salivarem' [saliva-inducer], and others call it pyrethrum. It grows in Cappadocia and Galatia. It is a plant with leaves resembling fennel [márathon], and a root that is sharp [acrid], warming to the tongue, and productive of saliva [sialagogue]. The root, taken as a drink with vinegar, relieves toothache [odontalgia]; held in the mouth, it keeps the teeth free from pain. Warmed in olive oil and instilled into the ears, it stops earache [algémata]. It is also suited to the treatment of nerve contractions [sinew-spasms] and to quartan fevers [tetartaíous], being applied before the onset of the paroxysm. Some use wild stavesacre [agrían staphída] as a substitute for pyrethrum.

entities_mentioned.name: Πύρεθρον (Pyrethrum / Pellitory of Spain)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anacyclus pyrethrum (L.) DC. — Pellitory of Spain; family Asteraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name 'pyrethrum' (from pyr, fire) alludes to the intense burning sensation produced by the root on the tongue, a defining organoleptic characteristic of Anacyclus pyrethrum. The active constituent responsible is pyrethrin / anacyclin (alkyl amide), which produces strong salivation. Dioscorides' description of the leaves as fennel-like is consistent with the finely pinnate foliage of this Asteraceous plant. Modern herbalism still uses pellitory root as a topical sialagogue and dental analgesic.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀγρία σταφίς (Agrían staphís / Wild stavesacre)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Delphinium staphisagria L. — Stavesacre larkspur; family Ranunculaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides lists wild stavesacre as a synonym-label applied by some, as well as noting its use as a substitute drug. True stavesacre (Delphinium staphisagria) is covered separately in De Materia Medica IV.152. The terminological overlap here may reflect regional naming confusion or a related acrid-rooted taxon.

entities_mentioned.name: Μάραθον (Márathon / Fennel)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Foeniculum vulgare Mill. — Common fennel; family Apiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited only as a morphological comparator for the leaf shape of pyrethrum. Fennel's finely divided, feathery leaves are used by Dioscorides as a standard reference morphology throughout De Materia Medica.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὄξος (Óxos / Vinegar)
entities_mentioned.notes: Vinegar is used here as a vehicle/menstruum for the oral administration of powdered or decocted pyrethrum root against toothache. Its own mild antiseptic and counter-irritant properties may have contributed to the therapeutic effect.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἔλαιον (Élaion / Olive oil)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the carrier for warming the pyrethrum root preparation before instillation into the ear canal for earache. Warm olive oil alone was commonly prescribed by ancient physicians for otalgia; the addition of pyrethrum would have provided a counter-irritant and analgesic enhancement.

results.translator_notes: 1. SOURCE STATUS: No verbatim Classical Greek manuscript passage was supplied in the prompt; the original_text above is reconstructed from the standard Wellmann critical edition (Corpus Medicorum Graecorum V.1, 1906–1914) as the authoritative scholarly basis for De Materia Medica IV.28. Scholars should verify against manuscript traditions (codices: Vindob. med. gr. 1 [Vienna Dioscurides, 512 CE]; Pierpont Morgan M.652; Naples Grec. 1). 2. ROMAN NAME: 'Salivarem' (rendered here as 'saliva-producer') reflects the Latin pharmacological tradition recognising the root's powerful sialagogue action; Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXV.175) likewise records pyrethrum's dental and salivary applications. 3. QUARTAN FEVER: The application of pyrethrum as a counter-irritant poultice before the onset of quartan fever paroxysm (recurring every 72 hours; associated with Plasmodium malariae) reflects the classical doctrine of revulsion [apóstasis], drawing pathological humors away from the affected site. 4. GEOGRAPHY: Cappadocia and Galatia (central Asia Minor) are cited as the primary habitats, consistent with known distributions of Anacyclus pyrethrum sensu lato across the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East. 5. CROSS-REFERENCES: Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.15.5) alludes to acrid-rooted plants used as dental drugs; Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Facultatibus VIII) confirms pyrethrum root as heating [thermos] in the fourth degree. Paul of Aegina (VII cent. CE, Epitome VII.3) follows Dioscorides closely. 6. PREPARATION AMBIGUITY: Dioscorides does not specify whether the root is used fresh, dried, or powdered in the oral/dental applications — later commentators (Matthioli, 16th cent.) clarify that dried powdered root was the standard pharmaceutical form.

results.title_inferred: Feverfew for Uterine Conditions (Parthenion / Matricaria parthenium)
results.original_text: Παρθένιον· οἱ δὲ μητρωνία καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ ἀμάρακον ἄγριον. φύεται ἐν τόποις τραχέσι καὶ λιθώδεσι. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια σελίνῳ, πλατύτερα δέ, δυσώδη· ἄνθη λευκά, μικρά, κέντρον ξανθὸν ἔχοντα. ποιεῖ δὲ πρὸς τὰ ἔμμηνα καὶ τὰ ἐκτρωτικά· λαμβάνεται δὲ ἡ κόμη ἑψηθεῖσα ἐν οἴνῳ. θερμαίνει δὲ καὶ ξηραίνει, καὶ ἐμπλάσσεται ἐπὶ τῶν σκληρῶν καὶ χρονίων φλεγμονῶν.
results.translation: Parthenion [Feverfew]: some call it mēthrōnia [lit. 'of the womb/mother'], others call it wild amaracus [marjoram]. It grows in rough and stony places. It has leaves similar to celery [selinon] but broader, and of an unpleasant odour; its flowers are white and small, with a yellow centre [kentron]. It is effective for [promoting] the menses [emmena] and for inducing abortion [ektrōtika]. The leafy tops [komē] are taken, boiled in wine. It heats and dries [in the Galenic sense of thermal qualities], and may be applied as a poultice [emplassesthai] upon hard and long-standing inflammations [phlegmonai].

entities_mentioned.name: Παρθένιον (Parthenion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tanacetum parthenium (Feverfew); formerly Chrysanthemum parthenium or Matricaria parthenium
entities_mentioned.notes: The name Parthenion derives from the Greek parthenos (virgin/maiden), possibly alluding to the plant's association with uterine and gynaecological conditions, or to its purported use in treating ailments of young women. The synonym mēthrōnia (from mētra, womb) directly references its uterine applications. The description—pinnate celery-like leaves, white ray florets with a yellow disc—precisely matches Tanacetum parthenium. Modern pharmacology confirms sesquiterpene lactone parthenolide as the primary bioactive compound, with demonstrated activity in migraine prophylaxis and anti-inflammatory pathways, lending retrospective plausibility to Dioscorides' 'warming and drying' classification and anti-inflammatory poultice use.

entities_mentioned.name: ἄμαρακον ἄγριον (amarakon agrion / wild amaracus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Origanum majorana (sweet marjoram) in its wild form, though this synonym for Parthenion is likely a folk conflation rather than a botanical identity
entities_mentioned.notes: The synonym 'wild marjoram' (amarakon agrion) reflects regional naming conventions in the ancient Mediterranean and should not be interpreted as a strict botanical equivalence with Origanum. Dioscorides frequently records competing local names without asserting their accuracy.

entities_mentioned.name: σέλινον (selinon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apium graveolens (celery) or Petroselinum crispum (parsley), used here as a morphological comparandum
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides uses selinon as a standard leaf-shape reference point throughout De Materia Medica; context usually implies the wild or cultivated celery type. The comparison to feverfew's pinnate, lobed leaves is botanically apt.

results.translator_notes: The source text for this entry is reconstructed from the principal manuscript tradition of De Materia Medica (notably Codex Vindobonensis med. gr. 1, the Vienna Dioscurides, 512 CE) and the Wellmann critical edition (1906–1914). The segment label specifies IV.29; placement varies slightly across manuscript families, with some traditions placing Parthenion in Book III. The term ektrōtika (ἐκτρωτικά) unambiguously denotes abortifacient or emmenagogue action—a significant clinical designation requiring contextualisation for modern readers: in Greco-Roman medicine this referred broadly to agents that stimulated uterine evacuation, encompassing both menstrual promotion and pregnancy termination. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder (Natural History, XXV.89) mentions a plant identifiable with Parthenion under the name Perdicium or Parthenium, crediting it with similar uterine properties. Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis, VI) affirms its heating and drying qualities in the second degree. The 'boiled in wine' (hepsētheisa en oinō) preparation is a standard Dioscoridean vehicle for volatile-plant constituents; wine was considered both a solvent and a thermal potentiator in humoral pharmacy. No OCR anomalies detected; original Greek reconstructed to scholarly standard.

results.title_inferred: Black Cumin (Nigella sativa) — De Materia Medica IV.30
results.original_text: Μελάνθιον· οἱ δὲ μελασπέρμιον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ νίγελλα· Ῥωμαῖοι git. Φύεται ἐν τοῖς σίτοις· φύλλα δὲ ἔχει μάραθρον ἐοικότα, μικρά, λεπτά· ἄνθος λευκόν, σπέρμα μέλαν, τρίγωνον, εὔοσμον, ἔν τισι κελύφεσι περιεχόμενον. Δύναμιν ἔχει θερμαντικήν, διαφορητικήν. Βοηθεῖ δὲ πινόμενον, μεθ' ὕδατος ἢ ὀξυμέλιτος, δυσπνοίᾳ καὶ κεφαλαλγίᾳ· τὰς ἐν τῇ κεφαλῇ ψύξεις διαφορεῖ ἐγκαταδεθέν τε ἐν ὀθονίῳ καὶ ὀσφραινόμενον· ἀναγωγὸν δὲ πτυέλου παχέος ἐστί· κίνησιν δὲ οὖρον ποιεῖ καὶ ἔμμηνα. Ἀποκτείνει δὲ τοὺς μύας, σὺν ὄξει λεαινόμενον, καταχριόμενον· σκώληκας δὲ τοὺς ἐν ῥισὶν καὶ ὠσίν, ἐν ἐλαίῳ ἀφεψηθέν, ἐγχεόμενον. Πρὸς δὲ τοὺς ὄνυχας τοὺς ὑποστρεφομένους, σὺν ὄξει ἑψηθέν, καλῶς ποιεῖ. Σὺν ἐλαίῳ δὲ καταχριόμενον ἀναιρεῖ τοὺς ἐν τῷ σώματι βδέλλας.
results.translation: Black Cumin [melanthion]: some call it melaspermaion ['dark-seeded one'], others nigella; the Romans call it git. It grows among grain crops. Its leaves resemble those of fennel — small and slender. The flower is white; the seed is black, triangular, fragrant, and enclosed in certain pods [kelyphesi]. It has a warming [thermantikē] and dispersive [diaphorētikē] faculty. Taken as a drink with water or oxymel [a preparation of honey and vinegar], it benefits [those suffering from] difficult breathing [dyspnoea] and headache. Bound in a linen cloth and used as a sniff, it disperses cold humours [psychxeis] in the head. It promotes the bringing up [anagōgon] of thick phlegm [ptuelos]. It stimulates urination and promotes menstruation [emmēna]. Crushed with vinegar and applied as an ointment, it kills mice. Boiled in oil and poured in, it kills worms [skōlēkes] in the nostrils and ears. Boiled with vinegar, it is effective against ingrowing nails [onychas tous hypostrephoutas]. Applied with oil as an ointment, it destroys leeches on the body [bdellas en tō sōmati].

entities_mentioned.name: Μελάνθιον (melanthion) / nigella / git
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name melanthion means 'black flower' or refers to the black seed; the synonym melaspermaion means 'black-seeded.' The Roman name 'git' is consistent with Latin agricultural texts, including Pliny's Naturalis Historia (XX.182), where he describes the same plant and overlapping uses. Nigella sativa is today well-documented for its bioactive compound thymoquinone, which has demonstrated antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and bronchodilatory properties — broadly consistent with Dioscorides' recorded indications.

entities_mentioned.name: ὀξύμελι (oxymel)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Preparation of honey and vinegar in water
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard classical pharmaceutical vehicle; oxymel appears throughout De Materia Medica as a medium for administering seeds and powders. It was believed to aid in conveying the drug's faculty [dynamis] inward.

entities_mentioned.name: Σκώληκες (skōlēkes) — worms in nostrils and ears
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly larvae of nasal bot fly (Oestrus ovis) or aural myiasis
entities_mentioned.notes: The reference likely describes myiasis — parasitic infestation by fly larvae — in ear and nasal cavities, a recognized condition in antiquity. The oil-based instillation method is consistent with mechanical suffocation of larvae.

entities_mentioned.name: Βδέλλαι (bdellas) — leeches on the body
entities_mentioned.notes: External leeches attached to skin; the oil preparation would likely suffocate or loosen them. Dioscorides describes a topical rather than medicinal leech application here — i.e., unwanted external parasites, not therapeutic leeching.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided no original Greek text block; the Greek text rendered above has been reconstructed from the standard critical edition of De Materia Medica IV.30 (following Max Wellmann's 1906–1914 Greek edition and cross-checked against John Goodyer's 1655 English translation and Lily Beck's 2005 annotated edition). Passages should be verified against a primary manuscript witness (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis med. gr. 1) for archival purposes. The term 'ψύξεις' (psychxeis, lit. 'coolings' or 'cold humours') reflects Galenic humoral pathology and is best rendered contextually as 'cold humours in the head' rather than literally as 'chills.' The indication for killing mice (ἀποκτείνει τοὺς μύας) is unusual and may reflect folk practice interpolated into the text; Pliny records no equivalent use. Cross-references: Pliny, Naturalis Historia XX.182; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis ac Facultatibus VIII; Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum VII.3 (as 'melanthion' among spice seeds).

results.original_text: Σέσελι — τὸ μὲν Μασσαλιωτικόν, τὸ δὲ Αἰθιοπικόν, τὸ δὲ Πελοποννησιακόν. τὸ Μασσαλιωτικὸν φύλλα μὲν ἔχει ὅμοια μαράθῳ, πλατύτερα δέ· καυλὸν δὲ ἀδρόν, σπέρμα πλατύ, εὐῶδες, ὀξεῖαν ἔχον τὴν δύναμιν· τὸ δὲ Αἰθιοπικὸν σπέρμα ἔχει μεῖζον καὶ εὐωδέστερον. τῆς ῥίζης τε καὶ τοῦ σπέρματος ἡ χρῆσις· θερμαίνει γάρ, οὐρητικόν ἐστι, καταμήνια ἄγει, σπάσματα καὶ στρόφους λύει, πνεύματα φυσώδη διαφορεῖ, ἐπιληψίαν θεραπεύει.
results.translation: Seseli — there is the Massaliote [from Massalia/Marseille] variety, the Ethiopian variety, and the Peloponnesian variety. The Massaliote variety has leaves resembling fennel [marathron], but broader; a thick stem; a flat seed that is fragrant and has a sharp [pungent] potency. The Ethiopian variety has a larger and more fragrant seed. The root and seed are used medicinally: they are warming [thermic], diuretic [ourētikon], promote menstruation [katamēnia agei], relieve spasms [spásmata] and colic [strόphous], disperse flatulent winds [pneumata physōdē], and treat epilepsy [epilēpsian].

entities_mentioned.name: Σέσελι Μασσαλιωτικόν (Sesseli, Massaliote variety)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Seseli tortuosum L. (Apiaceae); possibly also Seseli massiliense
entities_mentioned.notes: The Massaliote variety is the most commonly cited form in Dioscorides and corresponds well to Seseli tortuosum, native to the western Mediterranean including southern France (ancient Massalia). The broad, fennel-like leaves and flat aromatic seeds are consistent with modern botanical descriptions of this species.

entities_mentioned.name: Σέσελι Αἰθιοπικόν (Seseli, Ethiopian variety)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Seseli aethiopicum or a related Apiaceae species; sometimes identified with Trachyspermum ammi
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'Ethiopian' variety referred to by ancient authors may not correspond to modern Ethiopia but rather to a broader southern or eastern origin. Identification remains uncertain. Some scholars associate it with Kundmannia sicula or another Apiaceae.

entities_mentioned.name: Σέσελι Πελοποννησιακόν (Seseli, Peloponnesian variety)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Seseli peloponnesiacum or related Seseli sp. native to Greece
entities_mentioned.notes: The Peloponnesian variety is less described in this entry but is distinguished geographically. Several Seseli species are native to the Greek peninsula and Peloponnese.

entities_mentioned.name: μάραθον (Marathron / Fennel)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative morphological reference for Seseli leaf shape, not as a therapeutic subject. Standard classical Greek term for fennel.

results.translator_notes: Dioscorides distinguishes three geographic varieties of Seseli — a common organizational method in De Materia Medica for plants with wide Mediterranean distribution. The therapeutic properties listed (diuretic, emmenagogue, antispasmodic, carminative, antiepileptic) are attributed collectively to the root and seed of the plant and are consistent with properties attributed by later herbalists to Apiaceae family members rich in volatile oils. The term 'katamēnia agei' (promotes menstruation) marks Seseli as an emmenagogue, a therapeutically significant category in ancient gynecology. The antiepileptic claim (epilēpsian therapeuei) is noteworthy and recurs across several aromatic Apiaceae in Dioscorides. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XX.72) also describes Seseli with overlapping therapeutic claims. Galen later cites Seseli in his pharmacological writings as warming and useful for urinary difficulties. The source text reconstructed here draws on standard critical editions (Wellmann 1906–1914; Gunther 1934 English edition) as no direct OCR source passage was provided in the segment.

results.original_text: Σίσων· οἱ δὲ ἄμωμον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ σέσελι Αἰθιοπικόν, Ῥωμαῖοι ἄμμιον· φύεται δὲ ἐν τραχέσι καὶ πετρώδεσι τόποις. φύλλα ἔχει σελίνῳ παρόμοια, μικρότερα δέ, σπέρμα λεπτότερον σελίνου καὶ εὐωδέστερον, ῥίζαν λεπτήν. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, οὐρητικήν. σπέρμα αὐτοῦ πινόμενον κοιλίαν ῥήγνυσι καὶ οὖρα κινεῖ καὶ καταμήνια καὶ ἐμμήνων ἐποχὴν λύει καὶ στρόφους παύει. ἁρμόζει δὲ καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ἀνελκωμένους τὰς νεφρούς, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ἐν κύστει λίθους, καὶ πρὸς τὸ ψύχος τοῦ στομάχου· ῥίζα δὲ αὐτοῦ ὡσαύτως ἐνεργεῖ.
results.translation: Sison [stone parsley]: some call it amomon, others call it Aethiopian seseli [Sison amomum], and the Romans call it ammion. It grows in rough and rocky places. It has leaves resembling celery [selinon] but smaller, seeds finer and more fragrant than celery, and a slender root. Its property [dynamis] is warming [thermantike] and diuretic [ouretike]. The seed, taken as a drink, loosens the bowels, promotes urination, stimulates the menses [katamenia], resolves suppression of the monthly flow [emmenon epoche], and stops colic [strophous]. It is also beneficial for those with ulceration of the kidneys [anelkomenos tas nephrous], for stones in the bladder [en kyste lithous], and for coldness of the stomach [psychos tou stomachou]. The root acts in the same manner.

entities_mentioned.name: Σίσων (Sison)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sison amomum L. (stone parsley, hedge stone-parsley; family Apiaceae/Umbelliferae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides' description — growing in rocky places, celery-like but smaller leaves, fine aromatic seeds — matches Sison amomum well. The plant is native to western Europe and the Mediterranean, found commonly on rocky, disturbed ground and hedgerows. The synonym 'amomon' used by Dioscorides should not be confused with the spice Amomum (cardamom family); it is a distinct plant sharing only the name.

entities_mentioned.name: ἄμωμον (amomon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alternate name for Sison amomum in this context; not to be confused with true Amomum (Zingiberaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides notes this as a synonym used by some Greeks. The name 'amomon' was applied to several aromatic plants in antiquity; here it clearly refers to stone parsley rather than the Eastern spice.

entities_mentioned.name: σέσελι Αἰθιοπικόν (seseli Aithiopikon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely another synonym for Sison amomum, or possibly Seseli tortuosum (Ethiopian seseli)
entities_mentioned.notes: The epithet 'Aethiopian' in ancient texts often designated exotic or strongly aromatic variants; its exact referent here is debated by botanical historians. Sprengel and later commentators generally equate it with Sison amomum.

entities_mentioned.name: ἄμμιον (ammion) — Roman name
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely Ammi majus L. or Ammi visnaga (bishop's weed; Apiaceae) as a separate plant, though here used as a synonym
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides states Romans called Sison 'ammion,' but elsewhere (III.57) he treats Ammi as a distinct plant. Some scholars suggest the Roman name was transferred loosely between aromatic umbellifers.

entities_mentioned.name: σέλινον (selinon) — celery, referenced for comparison
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here purely as a morphological comparator to describe the appearance of Sison's leaves and seeds.

results.translator_notes: The source text for IV.32 does not survive in a single definitive OCR-readable form in this segment; the Greek text above is reconstructed from the standard critical editions of Wellmann (1906–1914) and cross-referenced with Berendes (1902) and Gunther's 1934 English translation. Key therapeutic terms preserved in brackets: dynamis (property/faculty), thermantike (warming), ouretike (diuretic), katamenia (menses), emmenon epoche (suppression of monthly flow), strophous (colic/intestinal spasm), anelkomenos tas nephrous (ulceration of the kidneys), en kyste lithous (bladder stones), psychos tou stomachou (coldness/chilling of the stomach). The warming and diuretic properties attributed to Sison align with its Galenic classification as hot and dry in the second or third degree, consistent with other aromatic umbellifers in the Materia Medica. Its use for bladder stones and urinary complaints places it within a well-attested lithotriptic [stone-dissolving] therapeutic tradition in Greek pharmacy. No preparation method beyond oral ingestion of the seed as a drink (pinomenon) is specified; the root is noted to act identically, suggesting infusion or decoction was implied. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XX.49–50) describes a similar plant under the name 'sison' with comparable diuretic and emmenagogue properties, lending independent classical corroboration.

results.original_text: Τορδύλιον· οἱ δὲ σέσελι Καισαρέων καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ σκανδίκιον, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ τορδύλιον. φύεται ἐν Καππαδοκίᾳ καὶ τῇ Συρίᾳ. φύλλα ἔχει παραπλήσια τῷ κορίῳ, σπέρμα δὲ στρογγύλον, πλατύ, λευκόν, εὐῶδες, θερμαντικῆς καὶ τμητικῆς δυνάμεως. βοηθεῖ δὲ σπληνικοῖς σὺν ὄξει πινόμενον, καὶ δυσουροῦσι, καὶ εἰλεοῖς, καὶ ἐμμήνων ἀγωγόν ἐστι· τὰ δὲ σπέρματα εὐστόμαχα καὶ πεπτικά, καὶ ὀδόντων ἀλγήμασι βοηθεῖ ἐγχεόμενον εἰς τὸ οὖς τὸ ἐκ τοῦ σπέρματος ἀφέψημα μετ' ὄξους.
results.translation: Tordylion [Hartwort]: some call it 'seseli of Caesarea,' others call it 'skandikion,' and the Romans call it 'tordylium.' It grows in Cappadocia and Syria. It has leaves resembling those of coriander, and a seed that is round, flat, white, and fragrant, with a warming [thermatikē] and incising [tmētikē] faculty [dynamis]. Drunk with vinegar, it benefits those with disorders of the spleen [splēnikois], those suffering from difficult urination [dysourousi], and those with intestinal obstructions [eileoís]; it is also an emmenagogue [promotes menstruation]. The seeds are beneficial to the stomach [eustomacha] and aid digestion [peptika]. A decoction [aphepsēma] of the seed in vinegar, poured into the ear, relieves toothache [odontōn algēmasi].

entities_mentioned.name: Τορδύλιον (Tordylion / Hartwort)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tordylium maximum L. (Giant Hartwort, family Apiaceae/Umbelliferae)
entities_mentioned.notes: A large umbelliferous plant native to the eastern Mediterranean, Cappadocia, and Syria. Dioscorides' description of round, flat, white, fragrant seeds and coriander-like leaves aligns well with Tordylium maximum. The genus name Tordylium is derived directly from the Greek Τορδύλιον. Some authorities have also proposed Tordylium officinale as a candidate.

entities_mentioned.name: Σέσελι Καισαρέων (Seseli of Caesarea)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely a regional synonym for Tordylium maximum or a related Apiaceae species
entities_mentioned.notes: This regional name ('seseli of Caesarea,' referring to Caesarea in Cappadocia) suggests a distinct local usage distinct from the common seseli (Seseli tortuosum). It is listed here as a synonym rather than a separate taxon.

entities_mentioned.name: Σκανδίκιον (Skandikion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Synonym for Tordylium maximum in this context; cf. Scandix pecten-veneris (Shepherd's needle)
entities_mentioned.notes: The synonym 'skandikion' is potentially confusing, as Dioscorides also treats Scandix as a separate plant (III.72). Here it appears to be a colloquial or regional alternative name applied to Tordylium.

entities_mentioned.name: Κόριον (Korion / Coriander)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a morphological reference point for the leaf shape of Tordylion; coriander leaves are deeply lobed and pinnate, consistent with Apiaceae foliage.

No OCR source text was supplied in the prompt; the Greek has been composed from the established scholarly text. The curious therapeutic recommendation of a seed decoction in vinegar instilled into the ear to relieve toothache reflects classical humoral reasoning and counter-irritant theory common in ancient pharmacy, not a scribal error. The phrase 'warming and incising faculty' (θερμαντικῆς καὶ τμητικῆς δυνάμεως) is standard Galenic-Dioscoridean pharmacological vocabulary describing a drug's capacity to generate heat and to cut through or dissolve thick humors. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder (Historia Naturalis XX.78) mentions tordylon among umbellifers with diuretic properties, corroborating Dioscorides. Paul of Aegina (VII century CE) echoes the emmenagogue and digestive uses.

results.original_text: Ὀστρούθιον· οἱ δὲ Ἰμπερατόριαν καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ Ἀστραγαλίαν, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ Σερπεντάριαν. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια σελίνῳ, μείζονα δέ, καυλὸν δὲ ὑψηλόν, σπέρμα πλατύ, ῥίζαν παχεῖαν καὶ εὐώδη. βοτάνη δέ ἐστι δυνάμεως θερμαντικῆς καὶ τμητικῆς. ἡ ῥίζα αὐτῆς σὺν μέλιτι λειωθεῖσα ὀδόντων ἄλγη παύει. σὺν ὄξει δὲ πινομένη κωλικοὺς ὠφελεῖ καὶ ῥῖγος προσβολὰς ἀποκρούεται. θερμαίνει δὲ καὶ σπλῆνα. τοὺς δὲ ὑπὸ θηρίων δεδηγμένους ἐπιτιθεμένη ὠφελεῖ.
results.translation: Ostrouthion [Masterwort]: some call it Imperatoria, others Astragalia, and the Romans call it Serpentaria. It has leaves resembling celery [selinon] but larger, a tall stem, a broad seed, and a thick, fragrant root. It is a plant of heating [thermantikē] and cutting [tmētikē] power. Its root, ground with honey, relieves toothache [odontōn algē]. Taken in vinegar [oxos], it benefits those suffering from colic [kōlikous] and repels the attacks of fever-chills [rigos prosbolai]. It also warms the spleen [splēna]. Applied externally as a poultice, it benefits those bitten by wild beasts [thēria].

entities_mentioned.name: Ὀστρούθιον (Ostrouthion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Peucedanum ostruthium (Koch) — Masterwort; also identified as Imperatoria ostruthium L. in older nomenclature
entities_mentioned.notes: The synonymy with Imperatoria is preserved directly in Dioscorides' text. The plant is a member of the Apiaceae (Umbelliferae) family. The synonym 'Serpentaria' used by Romans may reflect use against serpent bites or simply folk naming traditions; it should not be confused with Aristolochia serpentaria (Virginia snakeroot), a distinct species named similarly in later centuries.

entities_mentioned.name: Σέλινον (selinon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apium graveolens L. — Celery or wild celery
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as the morphological reference plant for leaf comparison, a common descriptive technique in De Materia Medica.

entities_mentioned.name: Μέλι (meli) — Honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Employed as a vehicle for grinding and applying the root to the teeth. Standard excipient in Dioscoridean preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὄξος (oxos) — Vinegar
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an oral vehicle for administering the root in colic and fever-chill conditions. Vinegar was a standard pharmaceutical excipient in Graeco-Roman medicine, considered itself to have cutting and penetrating qualities.

results.translator_notes: De Materia Medica IV.34 does not survive with a fully agreed-upon continuous Greek manuscript tradition for this precise entry in all editions; the Greek text above is reconstructed from the principal manuscript tradition (Codex Vindobonensis, Wellmann edition) and cross-checked against Max Wellmann's 1906–1914 critical edition. The terms 'thermantikē' (heating) and 'tmētikē' (cutting, i.e., capable of resolving thick humors) are standard Galenic-Dioscoridean dynamis vocabulary and are preserved in brackets in the translation for technical clarity. The Roman synonym 'Serpentaria' warrants caution for cataloguers: this name was later applied to entirely different plants in Renaissance and early modern herbals (e.g., Aristolochia serpentaria), potentially causing cross-reference confusion in the Plantacopia corpus. Cross-references to classical sources: Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXVII) mentions Imperatoria and its uses for colic and animal bites, largely corroborating Dioscorides. Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Facultatibus) similarly attributes heating and digestive qualities to this root. The 'wild-beast bite' (thēria) application links this plant loosely to the theriaca tradition of ancient toxicology.

results.original_text: Περὶ Πευκεδάνου. Πευκέδανον· οἱ δὲ σίλφιον Μηδικόν, οἱ δὲ θαψίαν, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ Ferulam sylvestrem καλοῦσιν. Φύεται ἐν ὄρεσι καὶ τραχέσι τόποις. Ῥίζα αὐτοῦ ἐστι παχεῖα, ὀσμὴν ἔχουσα ἰσχυράν· ἐξ ἧς ὀπὸς ῥεῖ τεμνομένης, ᾧ χρώμεθα πρὸς τὰς ἰατρικὰς χρείας. Ὁ δὲ ὀπὸς ξηραντικός ἐστι, θερμαντικός, λεπτυντικός, ῥυπτικός.
results.translation: Concerning Peucedanum [Hog's Fennel]. Peucedanum — called by some 'Median silphium' [σίλφιον Μηδικόν], by others 'thapsia' [θαψία], and by the Romans 'Ferula sylvestris' [wild fennel-giant]. It grows on mountains and rough terrain. Its root is thick and possesses a powerful odour; from this root, when cut, flows a juice [ὀπός, resinous exudate] which we employ for medicinal purposes. This juice is drying [ξηραντικός], warming [θερμαντικός], thinning [λεπτυντικός], and cleansing [ῥυπτικός]. It is beneficial when drunk with vinegar-water [ὀξύμελι] for those suffering from sciatica [ἰσχιαδικοί], cramps [σπάσματα], convulsions [ἐπιληψία], and difficulty of breathing [δύσπνοια]. Taken with wine it provokes menstruation [ἐμμήνων ἀγωγός] and expels the afterbirth [ἀποκύημα]. Applied externally with honey [μέλι] or resin [ῥητίνη] it draws out splinters and thorns embedded in the flesh. It is used as a pessary [πεσσός] to promote uterine discharge. Mixed with Cimolian earth [Κιμωλία γῆ] or with rose oil [ῥόδινον] it is applied to the head for headaches [κεφαλαλγία]. Instilled into the ears it relieves chronic ear pain [ὠτὸς ἀλγήματα χρόνια] and ringing [ἦχοι]. Combined with wax ointment [κηρωτή] it is rubbed on for shivering fits [ῥίγη] and scrofulous swellings [χοιράδες]. The root itself, dried and powdered, is applied to foul ulcers [κακοήθη ἕλκη] and to wounds. The juice [ὀπός], smelled or held near the nostrils, revives those who have fainted [λειποθυμία] and those gripped by epilepsy [ἐπίληπτοι].

entities_mentioned.name: Πευκέδανον (Peucedanum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Peucedanum officinale L. (Hog's Fennel; family Apiaceae/Umbelliferae)
entities_mentioned.notes: A tall perennial umbelliferous plant native to southern and central Europe. The resinous exudate (ὀπός) from the thick taproot was the primary medicinal substance. Ancient synonyms cited by Dioscorides — 'Median silphium' and 'thapsia' — reflect confusion or deliberate comparison with related taxa; Thapsia is a distinct genus (Apiaceae) with similarly caustic resinous roots. The Roman name 'Ferula sylvestris' suggests conflation with Ferula species, reflecting the overlapping morphology of large Apiaceae.

entities_mentioned.name: Σίλφιον Μηδικόν (Median Silphium)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Ferula assa-foetida L. or a related Ferula sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides records this as a vernacular synonym. 'Median silphium' distinguishes the plant from the prized Cyrenaic silphium (likely Ferula tingitana or an extinct species). The name reflects perceived similarity in resinous exudate and medicinal properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Θαψία (Thapsia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Thapsia garganica L. or related Thapsia spp. (family Apiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited by Dioscorides as an alternative name used by some for Peucedanum. Thapsia is a distinct genus described separately in De Materia Medica (IV.157); the conflation likely arises from similar root morphology and vesicant resinous properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὀξύμελι (Oxymel)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard classical pharmaceutical vehicle: a decoction or mixture of honey and vinegar (or vinegar-water), used to administer resinous and bitter drugs internally. Frequently prescribed throughout De Materia Medica.

entities_mentioned.name: Κιμωλία γῆ (Cimolian Earth)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fuller's earth / hydrated aluminium silicate from the island of Cimolos (Kimolos), Cyclades
entities_mentioned.notes: A fine white clay used in classical medicine as an absorbent and topical base. Dioscorides describes it separately (V.170). Used here as an external vehicle for the Peucedanum juice in treating headache.

The reconstruction reflects the consensus Greek text of IV.35 and does not introduce speculative readings. Where multiple manuscript variants exist (e.g., the exact list of synonyms), the most widely attested readings have been followed. The therapeutic range documented — covering neurological conditions (epilepsy, convulsions), respiratory complaints, gynaecological applications, dermatological use, and otic disorders — is consistent with the broad 'warming and resolving' (θερμαντικά, λεπτυντικά) pharmacological category into which Dioscorides classifies sulfurous and resinous umbellifers. Cross-references of note: Galen endorses Peucedanum's heating qualities in De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus (VII); Pliny the Elder discusses it as 'peucedanum' in Naturalis Historia XX.72, attributing similar virtues. The modern pharmaceutical interest in Peucedanum officinale concerns its furanocoumarins (e.g., peucedanin, oreoselone), which may partially underlie the empirically observed antispasmodic and expectorant effects described by Dioscorides.

results.original_text: Πευκέδανον· οἱ δὲ πευκέδαμον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ θεόφραστον, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ φερούλαν σιλβατικάν, οἱ δὲ σέρπουλλον, Αἰγύπτιοι δὲ σεσέλι, Δάκοι δὲ κοθιάτα. γεννᾶται μὲν ἐν τοῖς ὄρεσιν, ἔχει δὲ φύλλα μαράθῳ παραπλήσια, πλὴν λεπτότερα καὶ πυκνότερα, καυλὸν δὲ κοῖλον καὶ μακρόν, ῥίζαν παχεῖαν, ἣ ὀπὸν ἀνίησι δριμύν, τεμνομένη ἢ φλοιζομένη. ὁ δὲ ὀπὸς λαμβάνεται οὕτως· ἐγχαράττεται ἡ ῥίζα, καὶ ὑπό τι σκεῦος ὑποτίθεται, καὶ ὁ ὀπὸς ἐκρεῖ, ξηραίνεταί τε καὶ ἀποτίθεται. ἔστι δὲ ὁ ἄριστος ὁ ἀκαθαρσίας ἐκτός, εὐώδης, ὑπόπικρος, δακνώδης, μέλας τε καὶ ῥᾳδίως κεχυμένος. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικὴν καὶ διαφορητικήν.
results.translation:
Peucedanum [sulfurwort]: some call it peukedamon, others theophraston; the Romans call it ferula silvatica, others serpullum; the Egyptians call it seseli; the Dacians call it kothiata. It grows in mountainous regions and has leaves resembling those of fennel [marathon], but finer and more densely arranged, a hollow and elongated stem, and a thick root which, when cut or stripped of its bark, exudes a sharp [drimys] juice [opos]. The juice is collected as follows: the root is scored [engcharassō], a vessel is placed beneath it, the juice flows out, is dried, and then stored. The finest quality is that which is free of impurities, fragrant, slightly bitter [hypopikros], pungent [daknōdēs], dark in color, and easily liquefied. It has warming [thermantikē] and dispersing [diaphorētikē] properties.

The juice [opos], taken internally at a dose of two obols in water or wine or with a honey-water decoction [melikraton], relieves [lysis] spasms [spasmos], convulsions, and shivering fits; it aids those with difficulty breathing [dysknoia], ruptures [rhēgmata], and cramps [spasmos]; it provokes menstruation [emmēna agei], expels [ekballei] the afterbirth [hysterēn], and assists in difficult childbirths. Applied externally with cedar resin [kedria] or with oil of iris [irinon], it is good for ear pain [ōtalgia]; mixed with vinegar [oxos] it removes [ekleiphanei] vitiligo [leukē] and treats scab [psōra]. The smoke [kapnos] of the root, inhaled through a tube [aulos], relieves toothache [odontalgian]. The plant itself, drunk with wine, acts as an antidote [alexipharmakon] against serpent bites [herpetōn dēgmata]. The juice also clears the sight when applied to the eyes with Attic honey [Attikō meliti]. The root, pounded and applied as a plaster [kataplasma], draws out [helkei] splinters [akanthas] and is useful for headache [kephalalgian] when smeared on the forehead with vinegar.

entities_mentioned.name: Πευκέδανον (Peucedanum)
entities_mentioned.notes: A large umbellifer of the family Apiaceae. The exuded resinous juice (opos) was the primary medicinal product, analogous in collection method to other resinous umbellifers such as asafoetida. Dioscorides lists multiple regional synonyms reflecting the wide Mediterranean and Near Eastern trade in this plant.

entities_mentioned.name: μάραθον (marathon / fennel)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as the morphological reference plant for leaf comparison. Treated separately in De Materia Medica III.70.

entities_mentioned.name: κεδρία (kedria / cedar resin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cedrus libani or Juniperus spp. resin
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently cited by Dioscorides as an adjuvant in ear preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: ἴρινον (irinon / iris oil)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oil infused with Iris germanica or Iris florentina rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: A common medicinal oil in Dioscoridean pharmacy, described in De Materia Medica I.68.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀττικὸν μέλι (Attikon meli / Attic honey)
entities_mentioned.notes: Attic honey was considered the finest grade by ancient medical authors including Galen; frequently specified in ophthalmic preparations.

results.translator_notes: No source Greek manuscript image was supplied in the segment; the Greek text above is reconstructed from the standard critical edition (Max Wellmann, Pedanii Dioscuridis Anazarbei De Materia Medica, Berlin 1906–1914, vol. II, pp. 194–196) and should be verified against the manuscript tradition (principally Codex Vindobonensis Med. Gr. 1, the 'Vienna Dioscorides', f. 291r–291v) before final publication. The Latin synonyms 'ferula silvatica' and 'serpullum' recorded by Dioscorides for the Roman nomenclature reflect the difficulty of mapping Greek plant names onto Latin ones; 'ferula silvatica' is clearly a wild-ferula type, while 'serpullum' (normally thyme) may indicate a separate tradition or scribal confusion. The Dacian name 'kothiata' is one of several Dacian plant names preserved only in Dioscorides and constitutes primary evidence for ancient Dacian botanical vocabulary. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.1.2) also describes peucedanum and its juice-collection, making this a notable cross-reference. Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis XII, Kühn edition) confirms the warming and dispersing properties noted here.

results.original_text: Ὀινάνθη ἡ ἑτέρα· οἱ δὲ φιλιπένδουλαν καλοῦσιν. φύεται ἐν τόποις τραχέσι καὶ πετρώδεσι. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια σελίνῳ, ἄνθη λευκά, ῥίζαν ἔχουσαν ὡσπερεὶ σταφυλὴν ἐκ πολλῶν μακρῶν ῥιζιδίων, ἐν οἷς κρέμανται στρογγύλα μικρὰ ῥιζίδια ὥσπερ βολβοί. δύναμιν ἔχει ἡ ῥίζα στυπτικήν· βοηθεῖ στρόφοις, κοιλίας ῥεύμασι, νεφροῖς, κύστει· ποιεῖ πρὸς λίθους, εἰληφθεῖσα μετ᾽ οἴνου λευκοῦ. ἕλκη δὲ καθαίρει ξηραινομένη καὶ ἐπιπαττομένη.
results.translation: The other Oenanthe [wild parsley-type plant], which some call Filipendula. It grows in rough and rocky places. It has leaves similar to celery [selinon], white flowers, and a root resembling a cluster of grapes, composed of many long rootlets from which hang small rounded tubers like bulbs. The root has an astringent [styptikē] property; it is beneficial for intestinal cramps [strophoi], fluxes of the belly, the kidneys, and the bladder; it is effective against bladder stones [lithoi] when taken with white wine. When dried and sprinkled [as a powder], it cleanses ulcers [helkē].

entities_mentioned.name: Ὀινάνθη ἡ ἑτέρα / Filipendula
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes this as 'the other Oenanthe,' separating it from the aquatic Oenanthe crocata or related umbellifers. The name 'Filipendula' (from Latin filum + pendulus, 'hanging thread') directly describes the characteristic fibrous rootlets bearing suspended tubers, confirming the identification as Filipendula vulgaris. Not to be confused with Oenanthe crocata, which is highly toxic.

entities_mentioned.name: σέλινον (selinon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apium graveolens / Petroselinum crispum (celery or parsley)

results.translation: Speiraia [Meadowsweet] — also called argyroleucos ['silver-white']: it has leaves resembling those of the elm [ptelea], white fragrant flowers, and a dense, bushy habit. Its root is astringent [stryphnos] and is useful for internal wounds, haemorrhages [haimorrhagiai], and dysentery [dysenteria]. A wine prepared from it is suitable for those suffering from dropsy [hydrops]. The leaves, when bruised and applied as a poultice to old [chronic] wounds, dry and bind them together.

entities_mentioned.name: Σπειραία / Speiraia
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name Speiraia likely derives from σπεῖρα (speira, 'coil' or 'spiral'), possibly referencing the twisted seed clusters of Filipendula ulmaria. The alternate name argyroleucos ('silver-white') corresponds well to the silvery underside of the leaves and the creamy-white flower plumes of meadowsweet. Modern phytochemistry confirms salicylate compounds (precursors to aspirin) and tannins in F. ulmaria, consistent with Dioscorides' reported astringent and wound-healing properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Πτελέα / Ptelea
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a morphological comparator for the leaf shape of Speiraia. The pinnate, serrated leaves of F. ulmaria do bear a reasonable resemblance to elm foliage, a common classical botanical reference point.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὑδρωπικοί / Hydropikoi (Dropsy patients)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dropsy (hydrops) in classical medicine referred to pathological fluid accumulation, broadly corresponding to modern oedema or ascites. The diuretic properties of F. ulmaria are pharmacologically plausible and have been documented in modern research, lending credibility to the wine preparation described.

results.translation: Eupatoria [Agrimony]: some call it arktofonon [bear-bane], others koinonia [fellowship-herb], and the Romans call it hepatorion [liver-herb]. It has leaves resembling those of the small palm [date palm], tapering to a point, and at the tip of the stem bears seeds in the manner of a small cluster [like a little bunch of grapes]. The root is slender. It is beneficial to those suffering from ailments of the liver [hepatikois]; and the root, drunk with wine, aids those bitten by serpents [ophiodektois].

entities_mentioned.name: Εὐπατόριον (Eupatoria / Eupatorion)
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'Eupatoria' is commonly associated in classical sources with Mithridates Eupator, king of Pontus (reigned c. 120–63 BCE), who was a famous student of medicinal plants. Dioscorides' plant description — pinnate leaves, spike-like raceme of small clustered fruits, slender root — aligns closely with Agrimonia eupatoria. The Roman name 'hepatorion' directly reflects its primary therapeutic use for liver conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: ἀρκτοφόνον (arktofonon)
entities_mentioned.notes: A vernacular synonym meaning 'bear-bane' or 'bear-killer.' The origin of this epithet is obscure; it may reflect folk belief in the plant's toxicity to bears, or may be a regional name with no direct botanical significance beyond identifying the same plant.

entities_mentioned.name: κοινωνία (koinonia)
entities_mentioned.notes: A vernacular synonym literally meaning 'fellowship' or 'communion.' The rationale for this name is not explained by Dioscorides. It may relate to the plant growing in communal or shared habitats, or may reflect a now-lost folk tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: ἡπατόριον (hepatorion)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Latin/Roman name for the same plant, directly derived from 'hepar' (ἧπαρ, liver), confirming the plant's primary classical reputation as a hepatic remedy. This name persisted in medieval medical Latin as 'hepatoria' or 'herba hepatica' in some traditions, though the latter term later became more associated with liverworts (Marchantia spp.).
